original_abstract: Russia's invasion of Ukraine inspired a unity among Western democracies not seen since the first Gulf War. However, Western expectations of global unified condemnation and action against Russia were dashed by the response of the Global South. Far from endorsing the Western position, over 40 member states consistently abstained or voted against resolutions proposed in the UN General Assembly that sought to condemn Russian actions. In April 2022, in the wake of overwhelming evidence of human rights violations, 50 members voted against expelling Russia from the Human Rights Council. These included many African, Asian, Middle Eastern and Latin American countries, who were adamant that irrespective of who started it and how it was conducted, the most important response should be to bring the conflict to an immediate end. What is behind the seeming indifference and even hostility in the Global South to the Western position on Russia? This paper will review and assess how Russia's invasion of Ukraine has been seen through the lens of the Global South. It will examine how Global South countries viewed the Western response to Russia's invasion; ensuing debates over non-alignment generated by Russia's invasion amongst Global South countries; and, how the Russia-Ukraine war has reignited discussion on reform the multilateral system.
humanized_abstract: The invasion of Ukraine by Russia appears to have for the first time in a generation united Western democracies in a way not seen since the first Gulf War. The wider international community’s response to the invasion and the consequent measures called for by the West to deal with Russia was however a source of great disappointment. More than 40 countries stood alone in abstaining or voting against every resolution tabled in the UN General Assembly in defence of the Western position on Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. Even when the UN General Assembly voted in April 2022 to condemn evidence uncovered by inspectors of very serious violations of human rights that were found throughout Ukraine after Russia’s invasion, more than 50 members including a large number of countries from Africa, Asia, the Middle East and Latin America voted against Russia’s expulsion from the UN Human Rights Council on the basis that regardless of who instigated the war and how it was fought, the war itself must be ended immediately. This paper undertakes a review and analysis of the attitudes of the Global South towards Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, taking into account the attitudes of the Western response to Russia’s actions; the issues of non-alignment versus neutrality which arose in the Global South in relation to Russia’s actions; and, finally, how developments in relation to the war between Russia and Ukraine are influencing the renewed debates for reform of the multilateral system.
